title: Völuspá
stanzas: Hearing I ask from the holy races,
From Heimdall's sons, both high and low;
Thou wilt, Valfather, that well I relate
Old tales I remember of ages of old.
I remember yet the giants of yore,
Who gave me bread in the days of old;
Nine worlds I knew, nine home-trees I saw,
High in their branches, before the root.
Of old was the age when Ymir lived;
Sea nor cool waves nor sand there were;
Earth had not formed nor Heaven above,
But a yawning gap, and grass nowhere.
Then Bor's sons lifted the level land,
Made Midgard mild, and measurably large;
Sun from the south warmed the stone of powers,
And green with growing things grew the earth.
From the south the sun, by the side of the moon,
Flung his right hand over heaven's rim;
He knew not where his hall should be,
The stars knew not their proper place.
The gods then gathered to council wise,
The mighty powers in Iddavoll met;
Shrines they raised, and altars made,
Set up their smithies, stmelt the ore.
Gold they fashioned and fine ornaments,
Made the chess-men and measured the time,
Till the three Jotun-women came from the giant-kind,
Three maidens mighty in power they were.
Aesir and Vanir made war on each other,
They broke their oaths and the field was rife,
Then the Valkyries in battle-array
Chose from the slain the half for Odin.
I know how Odin gave his one eye
To the well of Mimir, the Mead to win,
I know where Odin his body laid down
On the gallows high, for nine long nights.
I know of the ash Yggdrasil great,
The high tree that holily feeds the race;
None know the length of its limbs stretched far,
Its roots lie beneath in the world so vast.
At the roots of Yggdrasil coils the serpent,
The Nidhogg eats the corpses there,
From Muspel come the maidens,
Radiating light throughout the worlds.
Then comes the doom of the powers great,
The mighty gods, and the end of all;
The sun grows dark and the stars are veiled,
The earth sinks down in the sea's domain.
Forth comes the snake, the serpent dire,
Breaking his bonds with a hideous roar,
The Fenrir-wolf breaks loose from his chains,
His jaws gape wide with terrible force.
The Bifrost bridge is cloven in twain,
The watchman of gods yields up his head,
The great tree shakes, and the stars are loosed,
The earth sinks down in the sea below.
From the south comes Surtr with sword of flame,
Brighter it shines than the sun itself,
He smites the gods in their stronghold proud,
And Heimdall meets him in struggle sore.
Great Odin falls to the Fenrir's fangs,
But Vidar avenges his father's death,
He sets his foot on the monster's jaw
And rends it apart in terrible strife.
The sun grows dark and the earth sinks down,
The stars are hidden and the sky splits asunder,
Steam rises up and the earth, all wet,
Again from the waves comes the world again.
